title: Manual Notes
raw_text:
6. Explain in brief 802.11 Architecture and Protocol Stack 
1. DefiniƟon / IntroducƟon
IEEE 802.11 is the standard used for Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs), commonly known as 
Wi-Fi. It defines how wireless devices communicate over radio waves and specifies the architecture 
and protocol layers used for wireless communicaƟon.
2. Main Body (Detailed & Pointwise) 
A. 802.11 Architecture 
The 802.11 architecture defines how wireless devices are organized and communicate within a 
wireless network. 
1. StaƟon (STA)
o A staƟon is any device that supports the 802.11 protocol. 
o Examples include laptops, smartphones, and tablets with Wi-Fi capability. 
2. Access Point (AP)
o An Access Point is a device that connects wireless staƟons to a wired network.
o It acts as a central communicaƟon point for wireless devices. 
3. Basic Service Set (BSS)
o A BSS is the basic building block of an 802.11 network. 
o It consists of one Access Point and mulƟple wireless staƟons. 
4. Extended Service Set (ESS)
o MulƟple BSS networks connected together form an ESS. 
o It allows users to move between access points while staying connected. 
5. DistribuƟon System (DS)
o The distribuƟon system connects mulƟple access points.
o Usually implemented using wired Ethernet networks. 
6. Portal
o A portal connects the wireless network to other networks such as the Internet. 
B. 802.11 Protocol Stack 
The 802.11 protocol stack defines the layers used for wireless communicaƟon.
1. Physical Layer (PHY)
o Responsible for transmiƫng and receiving signals through radio waves. 
o Defines frequency bands, modulaƟon techniques, and data transmission rates.
2. MAC Layer (Medium Access Control)
o Controls how devices access the wireless medium. 
o Handles frame transmission, error detecƟon, and collision avoidance. 
3. Logical Link Control (LLC)
o Provides interface between the MAC layer and higher network layers. 
o Ensures proper data flow between devices. 
4. Higher Layers (Network, Transport, ApplicaƟon)
o These layers handle rouƟng, data transport, and applicaƟons such as web browsing 
and email. 
3. Example 
A home Wi-Fi network where smartphones and laptops connect to the wireless router (Access 
Point) using the 802.11 standard is an example of 802.11 architecture. 
4. Conclusion 
The 802.11 architecture organizes wireless devices using components such as staƟons, access points, 
and service sets, while the protocol stack defines the layers responsible for wireless communicaƟon. 
Together they enable reliable wireless networking.

key_points: 802.11 Architecture The 802.11 architecture defines how wireless devices are organized and communicate within a wireless network.
Access Point (AP) o An Access Point is a device that connects wireless staƟons to a wired network.
o It acts as a central communicaƟon point for wireless devices.
o It consists of one Access Point and mulƟple wireless staƟons.
802.11 Protocol Stack The 802.11 protocol stack defines the layers used for wireless communicaƟon.

raw_text:
1.Compare Connec tion oriented and Connec tionless Protocols in Computer 
network. 
 
Basis Connection-Oriented Protocol Connectionless Protocol  
Connection 
Setup A connection must be established before 
data transmission begins. No connec tion is established before 
sending data. 
Reliability  Provides reliable communica tion and ensures 
data is delivered correctly. Does not guarantee reliable delivery 
of packets. 
Data Transfer  Data is sent in a con tinuous sequence a Ōer 
connection establishment.  Data is sent as independent packets 
called datagrams. 
Error Control  Includes error detec tion and correc tion 
mechanisms. Error control is generally not 
provided. 
Flow Control  Flow control mechanisms are used to 
regulate data transmission. Flow control is usually not 
implemented. 
Packet Order  Packets are delivered in the same order as 
sent. Packets may arrive out of order. 
Overhead  Higher overhead due to connec tion setup 
and management. Lower overhead because no 
connection setup is required.  
Examples  TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)  UDP (User Datagram Protocol)  
 
 
2)Explain the Applica tions of Computer Networks  
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
Computer networks are used to connect mul tiple computers and devices together  so that they can 
communicate, share data, and share resources efficiently. Networks play an important role in 
businesses, educa tion, communica tion, and daily life.  
 
2. Main Body (Detailed & Pointwise) 
1. Resource Sharing 
 One of the major applica tions of computer networks is sharing hardware and so Ōware 
resources  among mul tiple users.  
 Devices such as printers, scanners, and storage devices  can be shared by many computers 
connected to the same network. 
 This reduces cost and improves efficiency because the same resource can be used by many 
users. 
2. Communica tion 
 Networks allow people to communicate easily and quickly  with others across the world. 
 Communica tion can take place through email, messaging, video conferencing, and voice 
calls. 
 It helps organiza tions and individuals exchange informa tion instantly.  
3. Data and File Sharing 
 Computer networks enable users to share files, documents, and databases  between 
different computers. 
 Employees in an organiza tion can access and update the same data stored on a central 
server. 
 This makes collabora tion easier and improves produc tivity. 
4. Access to Remote Informa tion 
 Networks allow users to access informa tion from remote computers or servers  located 
anywhere in the world. 
 The Internet is the best example where people can access websites, research materials, and 
online services. 
 This makes informa tion easily available to everyone.  
5. Entertainment and Mul timedia 
 Computer networks are widely used for online entertainment . 
 Users can watch movies, listen to music, play online games, and stream videos using network 
connections. 
 Services like online gaming and streaming pla ƞorms depend heavily on networks.  
6. E-Commerce and Online Services 
 Networks enable online shopping, banking, ticket booking, and digital payments . 
 Businesses use computer networks to sell products and services through websites. 
 Customers can buy goods and services from anywhere using the Internet. 
 
3. Example 
For example, in a college network , students can access study materials from a server, share files with 
classmates, and use a shared printer through the network. 
 
4. Conclusion 
Computer networks are essen tial in modern life because they enable communica tion, resource 
sharing, and access to informa tion. They support many ac tivities such as educa tion, business 
operations, and online services.  
 
 
3)Describe the Different Network Hardware used in Computer Networks 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
Network hardware refers to the physical devices used to connect computers and other devices in a 
network. These devices help in transmi ƫng data and enabling communica tion between different 
systems. 
 
2. Main Body (Detailed & Pointwise) 
1. Network Interface Card (NIC) 
 A Network Interface Card  is a hardware component that allows a computer to connect to a 
network. 
 It provides a physical interface between the computer and the network cable or wireless 
network. 
 Each NIC has a unique MAC address  used for iden tifying the device on the network.  
2. Hub 
 A Hub is a simple networking device used to connect mul tiple computers in a LAN.  
 It receives data from one device and broadcasts it to all other connected devices . 
 Hubs operate at the physical layer  of the network and do not filter or manage traffic. 
3. Switch 
 A Switch connects mul tiple devices within a network and improves network performance.  
 Unlike hubs, a switch sends data only to the intended device  using MAC addresses. 
 It helps reduce network traffic and improves communica tion efficiency.  
4. Router  
 A Router is used to connect different networks together.  
 It forwards data packets between networks  based on IP addresses.  
 Routers are commonly used to connect local networks to the Internet . 
5. Bridge 
 A Bridge is used to connect two similar network segments.  
 It filters traffic based on MAC addresses  and forwards data only when necessary.  
 Bridges help in reducing network conges tion. 
6. Gateway  
 A Gateway is a device that connects two different networks that use different protocols . 
 It acts as a translator  between different network systems.  
 Gateways are o Ōen used to connect LANs with other types of networks . 
7. Modem  
 A Modem (Modulator-Demodulator)  is used to connect a computer or network to the 
Internet. 
 It converts digital signals from a computer into analog signals  for transmission over 
telephone lines and vice versa.  
 Modems are commonly used in home Internet connec tions. 
 
3. Example  
For example, in a home network, a router connects mul tiple devices like laptops and smartphones 
to the Internet , while a NIC in each device allows it to communicate with the network . 
 
4. Conclusion  
Network hardware devices such as NICs, hubs, switches, routers, bridges, gateways, and modems  
play an important role in establishing communica tion between computers and ensuring efficient data 
transfer in a network.  
 
4)Differen tiate between OSI and TCP/IP model.  
Basis OSI Model  TCP/IP Model  
Full Form  Open Systems Interconnec tion Model  Transmission Control Protocol / 
Internet Protocol Model  
Number of 
Layers Consists of 7 layers. Consists of 4 layers. 
Layers Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, 
Session, Presenta tion, Applica tion. Network Interface, Internet, 
Transport, Applica tion. 

Basis OSI Model TCP/IP Model 
Development  Developed by ISO (Interna tional 
Organization for Standardiza tion). Developed by DARPA (U.S. 
Department of Defense) . 
Concept It is a reference model  mainly used for 
understanding network communica tion. It is a practical model  used in real-
world networking and the Internet. 
Layer Structure  Clearly separates services, interfaces, and 
protocols . Does not clearly dis tinguish between 
these concepts. 
Use Mainly used for educational and 
conceptual purposes . Widely used in actual network 
communica tion and Internet 
protocols . 
Protocol 
Dependency  Protocol independent model. Based on standard protocols such as 
TCP and IP . 
 
 
5)Explain Network So Ōware with respect to Protocol Hierarchy and Design 
Issues for Layers 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
Network so Ōware refers to the set of programs and protocols that control communica tion between 
computers in a network . To simplify communica tion, network so Ōware is organized in the form of 
layers known as protocol hierarchy . 
 
2. Main Body (Detailed & Pointwise) 
1. Protocol Hierarchy 
 In computer networks, communica tion tasks are divided into multiple layers , where each 
layer performs a specific func tion. 
 This layered structure is known as protocol hierarchy . 
 Each layer provides services to the layer above it  and receives services from the layer below 
it. 
2. Purpose of Layering 
 Layering helps in reducing complexity  in network design. 
 Each layer can be developed and modified independently  without affec ting other layers.  
 It also helps in standardiza tion and easier troubleshoo ting of network problems. 
3. Communica tion between Layers  
 Communica tion occurs between corresponding layers of different computers  using 
protocols. 
 Data passes down through the layers at the sender side  and up through the layers at the 
receiver side . 
 Each layer adds its own control informa tion (headers)  to the data. 
4. Design Issues for Layers 
When designing network layers, several important issues must be considered: 
 Addressing:  Each device in a network must have a unique address to iden tify the sender and 
receiver. 
 Error Control:  Mechanisms should be provided to detect and correct errors during data 
transmission. 
 Flow Control:  The system should ensure that the sender does not send data faster than the 
receiver can process it. 
 Routing: The network must determine the best path for data to travel from source to 
destination. 
 Multiplexing: Multiple communica tion sessions should be allowed to use the same network 
resources efficiently.  
5. Advantages of Protocol Hierarchy  
 It provides organized communica tion structure . 
 It simplifies network development and maintenance . 
 It allows different vendors to develop compa tible networking systems . 
 
3. Example  
For example, in the OSI model , communica tion is divided into seven layers , where each layer 
performs specific networking func tions. 
     
     Unit 2 
6. Explain in brief 802.11 Architecture and Protocol Stack  
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
IEEE 802.11  is the standard used for Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs) , commonly known as 
Wi-Fi. It defines how wireless devices communicate over radio waves and specifies the architecture 
and protocol layers used for wireless communica tion. 
 
2. Main Body (Detailed & Pointwise)  
A. 802.11 Architecture  
The 802.11 architecture  defines how wireless devices are organized and communicate within a 
wireless network.  
1. Station (STA) 
o A station is any device that supports the 802.11 protocol.  
o Examples include laptops, smartphones, and tablets with Wi-Fi capability.  
2. Access Point (AP)  
o An Access Point  is a device that connects wireless sta tions to a wired network.  
o It acts as a central communica tion point for wireless devices.  
3. Basic Service Set (BSS)  
o A BSS is the basic building block of an 802.11 network.  

o It consists of one Access Point and mul tiple wireless sta tions. 
4. Extended Service Set (ESS)  
o Multiple BSS networks connected together form an ESS. 
o It allows users to move between access points while staying connected . 
5. Distribution System (DS)  
o The distribution system  connects mul tiple access points.  
o Usually implemented using wired Ethernet networks . 
6. Portal 
o A portal connects the wireless network to other networks such as the Internet. 
 
B. 802.11 Protocol Stack 
The 802.11 protocol stack  defines the layers used for wireless communica tion. 
1. Physical Layer (PHY)  
o Responsible for transmiƫng and receiving signals through radio waves . 
o Defines frequency bands, modula tion techniques, and data transmission rates.  
2. MAC Layer (Medium Access Control)  
o Controls how devices access the wireless medium. 
o Handles frame transmission, error detec tion, and collision avoidance . 
3. Logical Link Control (LLC)  
o Provides interface between the MAC layer and higher network layers . 
o Ensures proper data flow between devices. 
4. Higher Layers (Network, Transport, Applica tion) 
o These layers handle routing, data transport, and applica tions such as web browsing 
and email. 
 
3. Example 
A home Wi-Fi network  where smartphones and laptops connect to the wireless router (Access 
Point) using the 802.11 standard  is an example of 802.11 architecture. 
 
4. Conclusion 
The 802.11 architecture  organizes wireless devices using components such as sta tions, access points, 
and service sets, while the protocol stack  defines the layers responsible for wireless communica tion. 
Together they enable reliable wireless networking. 
 
 
 
 
 
8. Compare Token Ring and FDDI with their Frame Format  
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
Token Ring  and FDDI (Fiber Distributed Data Interface)  are LAN technologies that use a token 
passing method  to control access to the network. In this method, a special frame called a token 
circulates in the network and only the device holding the token can transmit data.  
 
2. Main Body (Comparison in Tabular Form)  
Basis Token Ring  FDDI 
Standard Defined by IEEE 802.5  standard.  Based on ANSI standard  for high-
speed networks.  
Transmission 
Medium Uses twisted pair cable . Uses optical fiber cable . 
Speed Typical speed is 4 Mbps or 16 Mbps . Provides higher speed up to 100 
Mbps. 
Network 
Structure  Uses a ring topology  where devices are 
connected in a circular form.  Uses dual ring topology  (primary and 
secondary ring).  
Token Passing  A single token circulates in the ring and the 
station with the token can transmit data.  Also uses token passing , but supports 
higher reliability with dual rings.  
Reliability  Less reliable compared to FDDI.  More reliable due to dual ring 
structure for fault tolerance . 
Cost Relatively less expensive . More expensive  due to fiber op tic 
technology.  
 
Frame Format  
Token Ring Frame Format  
The Token Ring frame contains the following fields:  
 Start Delimiter (SD):  Indicates the start of the frame.  

 Access Control (AC):  Controls token passing and priority. 
 Frame Control (FC):  Specifies whether the frame contains data or control informa tion. 
 Destination Address (DA):  Address of the receiving sta tion. 
 Source Address (SA):  Address of the sending sta tion. 
 Data: Actual data being transmi Ʃed. 
 Frame Check Sequence (FCS):  Used for error detec tion. 
 End Delimiter (ED):  Indicates the end of the frame. 
 Frame Status (FS):  Shows whether the frame was received successfully. 
FDDI Frame Format 
The FDDI frame consists of the following fields: 
 Preamble:  Used for synchroniza tion. 
 Start Delimiter (SD):  Indicates the start of the frame. 
 Frame Control (FC):  Defines the frame type. 
 Destination Address (DA):  Address of the receiver. 
 Source Address (SA):  Address of the sender. 
 Data: Contains the informa tion being transmi Ʃed. 
 Frame Check Sequence (FCS):  Used for error detec tion. 
 End Delimiter (ED):  Indicates the end of the frame. 
 
 
9. Write a short note on: Wi-MAX and RFID 
A) Wi-MAX 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
Wi-MAX (Worldwide Interoperability for Microwave Access)  is a wireless communica tion 
technology used to provide high-speed broadband internet over long distances . It is based on the 
IEEE 802.16 standard . 
2. Main Body (Detailed & Pointwise) 
1. Wireless Broadband Technology  
o Wi-MAX provides high-speed wireless internet access  to large areas. 
o It can cover cities, towns, and rural areas  where wired networks are difficult to 
install. 
2. Long Range Communica tion 
o Wi-MAX can provide connec tivity up to 30-50 kilometers  depending on the 
environment. 
o This makes it useful for metropolitan area networks (MANs) . 
3. High Data Transfer Speed  
o It supports high data transmission rates , enabling fast internet services. 
o It is suitable for video streaming, VoIP, and broadband services . 
4. Architecture  
o A Wi-MAX network consists of base stations and subscriber sta tions. 
o The base sta tion provides wireless connec tivity to mul tiple users.  
5. Advantages  
o Wide coverage area 
o High bandwidth 
o Useful in areas without wired infrastructure 
3. Example 
Providing wireless broadband internet to rural areas  using Wi- MAX base sta tions. 
4. Conclusion 
Wi-MAX is an important broadband wireless technology  that provides high-speed internet access 
over large distances. 
 
B) RFID 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
RFID (Radio Frequency Iden tification) is a technology used to identify and track objects using radio 
waves. It uses special tags a Ʃached to objects and a reader device to detect them.  
2. Main Body (Detailed & Pointwise) 
1. Components of RFID System  
o An RFID system mainly consists of RFID tags, RFID reader, and antenna . 
o These components work together to iden tify objects.  
2. RFID Tag 
o A tag contains a microchip and antenna . 
o It stores informa tion about the object to which it is a Ʃached. 
3. RFID Reader  
o The reader sends radio signals  to detect the RFID tag. 
o It receives data from the tag and transfers it to the computer system. 
4. Wireless Iden tification 
o RFID does not require direct contact or line of sight  to read the tag. 
o Multiple tags can be read at the same time. 
5. Advantages  
o Fast identification 
o Automatic tracking  
o Improved inventory management 
3. Example 
RFID is commonly used in inventory management in warehouses and libraries  to track items. 
4. Conclusion 
RFID technology enables automatic identification and tracking of objects  using radio signals and is 
widely used in many industries. 
 
 
10. Compare the Wireless Technologies: Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, and Wi-MAX 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, and Wi-MAX  are wireless communica tion technologies used to connect devices 
without cables. They differ in range, speed, coverage area, and applica tions. 
 
2. Main Body (Comparison in Tabular Form) 
Basis Bluetooth  Wi-Fi Wi-MAX 
Standard IEEE 802.15.1 IEEE 802.11 IEEE 802.16 
Coverage 
Range Very short range (about 10 
meters) Medium range (about 
100 meters ) Very long range (up to 30-
50 km) 
Data Speed  Low data transfer rate High data transfer rate Very high broadband speed  
Network Type  Personal Area Network 
(PAN) Local Area Network ( LAN) Metropolitan Area Network 
(MAN) 
Basis Bluetooth Wi-Fi Wi-MAX 
Power 
Consump tion Very low power  Moderate power 
consumption Higher power consump tion 
Devices 
Connected  Connects devices like 
headphones, keyboards, 
smartphones  Connects laptops, 
smartphones, routers, 
tablets Connects large areas such 
as cities and rural 
broadband networks  
Main Use  Short-range device 
communica tion Wireless internet access 
in homes, offices, and 
campuses Broadband wireless 
internet for large areas 
 
3. Example 
 Bluetooth:  Connecting wireless headphones to a smartphone.  
 Wi-Fi: Using a wireless router to access the internet in a home or college. 
 Wi-MAX:  Providing broadband internet to a city or rural area. 
 
Unit 3 
 
11. What is Framing in Network? Explain Byte Stuffing and Byte Count 
Framing Methods. 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
Framing is a technique used in the Data Link Layer  to divide the con tinuous stream of bits received 
from the network layer into smaller units called frames . These frames are transmi Ʃed over the 
network so that the receiver can correctly iden tify the start and end of each data unit . 
2. Main Body 
Framing 
 The data link layer breaks the stream of bits into manageable frames  for transmission. 
 Each frame contains header, data, and trailer fields  which help in addressing and error 
detection. 
 Framing helps the receiver synchronize with the sender and correctly interpret the data . 
Byte Count Method 
 In this method, the header of the frame contains a field that specifies the number of bytes 
in the frame . 
 The receiver reads this byte count to determine where the frame begins and where it ends . 
 This method is simple and easy to implement . 
 However, if the byte count field gets corrupted during transmission , the receiver may lose 
synchroniza tion and may not correctly iden tify the frame boundary.  
Byte Stuffing Method 
 In this method, frames start and end with a special flag byte . 
 If the same flag byte appears in the data por tion, an escape byte (ESC)  is inserted before it. 
 At the receiving end, the escape byte is removed to restore the original data. 
 This technique ensures that special flag bytes inside the data are not mistaken as frame 
boundaries . 
3. Example 
For example, if the flag byte is 01111110 , the sender inserts an escape character before the same 
paƩern appearing in the data field . 
4. Conclusion 
Framing is essen tial for organizing transmi Ʃed data into structured units , allowing the receiver to 
identify frame boundaries and process the data correctly.  
 
 
 
12. Discuss Error Detec tion and Correc tion Codes  
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
Error detec tion and correc tion codes are techniques used in computer networks to identify and 
correct errors  that may occur during data transmission due to noise or interference.  
2. Main Body  
Error Detec tion Codes  
These techniques help the receiver determine whether the received data contains errors . 
1. Parity Check  
 A parity bit  is added to the data bits to make the total number of 1s either even or odd . 
 If the number of bits changes during transmission, the receiver detects an error.  
 It is simple but can detect only single-bit errors . 
2. Checksum  
 The data is divided into fixed-size segments  and added together.  
 The result is transmi Ʃed as a checksum value  along with the data.  
 The receiver calculates the checksum again and compares it with the received value to 
detect errors.  
3. Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC)  
 CRC uses binary division with a generator polynomial . 
 The remainder obtained a Ōer division is added to the frame before transmission.  
 At the receiver side, the same calcula tion is performed to check whether the data has been 
altered. 
Error Correc tion Codes  
 These codes not only detect errors but also correct them automa tically. 
 One common technique is Hamming Code , which can locate the posi tion of an error and 
correct it.  
3. Example  
Hamming code  can detect and correct single-bit errors  in transmi Ʃed data. 
4. Conclusion  

Error detec tion and correc tion techniques help maintain data integrity and reliable communica tion 
in computer networks. 
 
 
13. Describe and Illustrate Byte Count and Bit Stuffing Framing Method 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
In computer networks, framing methods  such as byte count and bit stuffing  are used to iden tify the 
start and end of frames  during data transmission. 
2. Main Body 
Byte Count Method 
 In this method, the first field of the frame header contains the length of the frame . 
 This length specifies the number of bytes present in the frame . 
 The receiver reads this value and counts the number of bytes to determine the end of the 
frame. 
 The method is simple but if the length field is corrupted, the frame boundary becomes 
incorrect. 
Bit Stuffing Method 
 In this technique, frames are separated by special flag bits . 
 A common flag pa Ʃern used is 01111110 . 
 If the sender encounters five consecu tive 1s in the data , it automa tically inserts a 0 bit aŌer 
them. 
 This prevents the data from being mistaken as a flag sequence. 
 The receiver removes the extra inserted bits to restore the original data. 
3. Example 
If the data contains 011111, the sender inserts a 0 bit, making it 0111110. 
4. Conclusion 
Both byte count and bit stuffing  methods help in iden tifying frame boundaries and ensure correct 
data transmission in the data link layer. 
 
 
14. Explain Design Issues of Data Link Layer 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
The Data Link Layer  is responsible for providing reliable data transfer between two directly 
connected nodes . Several design issues must be considered to ensure efficient communica tion. 
2. Main Body 
1. Services to Network Layer 
 The data link layer provides services to the network layer . 
 These services may include unacknowledged connec tionless service, acknowledged 
connectionless service, and acknowledged connec tion-oriented service . 
2. Framing 
 The layer must divide the stream of bits into frames before transmission. 
 Framing helps the receiver identify the beginning and end of each message . 
3. Error Control 
 Errors may occur due to noise or interference in the communica tion channel . 
 The data link layer detects and some times corrects these errors using techniques such as 
CRC or parity check . 
4. Flow Control 
 Flow control ensures that the sender does not send data faster than the receiver can 
process it . 
 Techniques such as Stop-and-Wait protocol  are used for this purpose. 
5. Physical Addressing 
 Each frame contains MAC addresses  of the sender and receiver. 
 This helps in iden tifying the correct des tination device in the network.  
3. Example 
In Ethernet networks , the data link layer uses MAC addresses to deliver frames to the correct 
device. 
4. Conclusion 
Design issues of the data link layer ensure efficient, reliable, and organized data transmission 
between devices . 
 
 
15. Explain Ethernet Protocol and its Use in Network with Example 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
Ethernet is one of the most widely used LAN technologies  for connec ting computers in a network. It 
is defined by the IEEE 802.3 standard . 
2. Main Body 
Working of Ethernet 
 Ethernet uses the CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Mul tiple Access with Collision Detec tion) 
method for data transmission. 
 Before sending data, a device checks whether the communica tion channel is free . 
 If two devices transmit at the same time, a collision occurs , and the devices wait for a 
random time before retransmi ƫng. 
Features of Ethernet 
 Provides high-speed communica tion in local networks. 
 Uses MAC addresses  for identifying devices.  
 Supports different data rates such as 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, and 1 Gbps . 
Frame Structure 
 Ethernet frames contain destination address, source address, data field, and error checking 
field. 
3. Example 
Ethernet is commonly used in home and office LAN networks  where computers are connected 
through switches and cables . 
4. Conclusion 
Ethernet protocol provides reliable and efficient communica tion in local area networks , making it 
one of the most popular networking technologies. 
 
 
16. Explain X.25 Protocol with its Frame 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
X.25 is a packet-switched network protocol  used for communica tion in Wide Area Networks 
(WANs). It was developed by the International Telecommunica tion Union (ITU -T) to provide reliable 
data transmission over public data networks . X.25 ensures reliable communica tion by using error 
control, flow control, and virtual circuits . 
 
2. Main Body 
1. Characteris tics of X.25 Protocol  
 X.25 is a connection-oriented protocol , which means a connec tion must be established 
before data transmission begins. 
 Data is transmi Ʃed in the form of small packets  instead of a con tinuous data stream.  
 It provides error detec tion and correc tion mechanisms  to ensure reliable communica tion. 
 The protocol works well even on noisy communica tion lines. 
2. Layers in X.25 Protocol 
The X.25 protocol works mainly at three layers of the OSI model : 
1. Physical Layer  
o Responsible for transmiƫng raw bits  over the communica tion channel.  
o Defines the electrical and mechanical characteris tics of the connec tion. 
2. Data Link Layer  
o Ensures reliable data transfer between two nodes . 
o Uses protocols such as LAPB (Link Access Procedure Balanced)  for error control and 
flow control. 
3. Network Layer  
o Responsible for packet rou ting and logical communica tion between the source and 
destination. 
o Manages virtual circuits  and packet delivery. 
3. Virtual Circuits in X.25 
X.25 communica tion is based on virtual circuits , which provide a logical connec tion between two 
devices. 
Types of virtual circuits: 
 Switched Virtual Circuit (SVC)  
o A temporary connec tion established for a par ticular communica tion session.  
o It is terminated a Ōer the communica tion is completed.  
 Permanent Virtual Circuit (PVC)  
o A permanent connec tion established between two devices.  
o It remains ac tive continuously. 
4. X.25 Frame Format 
An X.25 packet (frame) consists of several fields used for communica tion. 
Fields of X.25 Frame  
1. Header 
o Contains control and addressing informa tion. 
o Identifies the virtual circuit and packet type.  
2. Logical Channel Iden tifier (LCI) 
o Identifies the virtual circuit number  used for communica tion. 
3. Control Field  
o Used for flow control and error management . 
4. Data Field  
o Contains the actual data being transmi Ʃed. 
5. Error Detec tion Field 
o Used to detect transmission errors. 
These fields help ensure that packets are delivered correctly and in sequence . 
 
3. Example 
X.25 networks were widely used in banking systems, airline reserva tion systems, and early packet-
switched data networks  because they provided high reliability even over low-quality 
communica tion lines. 
 
 
17. Draw the Frame Format for 802.3 Protocol and Explain Func tion of Each 
Field 
1. Definition / Introduc tion 
The IEEE 802.3 protocol  defines the Ethernet frame format  used in local area networks for 
transmiƫng data between devices.  
2. Main Body  
Fields of 802.3 Frame  
1. Preamble  
 A sequence of bits used for synchroniza tion between sender and receiver . 
2. Start Frame Delimiter (SFD)  
 Indicates the start of the frame . 
3. Destination Address  
 Specifies the MAC address of the receiving device . 
4. Source Address  
 Specifies the MAC address of the sending device . 
5. Length Field  
 Indicates the length of the data field  in the frame.  
6. Data Field  
 Contains the actual data being transmi Ʃed. 
7. Frame Check Sequence (FCS)  
 Used for error detec tion using CRC . 
3. Example  
In an Ethernet LAN , when a computer sends data to another computer, the data is transmi Ʃed in the 
802.3 frame format . 
4. Conclusion  
The 802.3 frame format organizes data into structured fields, ensuring accurate transmission and 
error detec tion in Ethernet networks .
